summary: All five action_* function signatures are incompatible with the Evaluation type alias and with execute()'s call pattern at line 181
failure_scenario: action_review (line 105), action_revise (line 68), action_publish (line 83), and action_delete (line 96) all take three positional args (extra, context, storage), while action_create (line 56) takes only one (storage). The Evaluation type at line 50 is Callable[[Extra, ArticleId], ArticleId] (two args), and execute() at line 181 calls evaluate(extra, context) with two. If lifecycle.resolve(action) returns any of these action_* functions directly (which the naming and module-level placement strongly suggests), calling execute('review', ...) raises TypeError: missing 1 required positional argument: 'storage'.

summary: Review is @dataclass(frozen=True) but contains scores: dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict) — the generated __hash__ includes the dict field, making Review unhashable
failure_scenario: hash(Review(...)) raises TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict'. Any use of Review as a dict key or in a set crashes at runtime. Despite frozen=True, review.scores['clarity'] = 5.0 also works silently (the dict reference is frozen, its contents are not).

summary: Article.score: dict[str, float] | None = None in a @dataclass(frozen=True) class — the same unhashable-type footgun
failure_scenario: When score is set to a non-None dict, hash(Article(...)) raises TypeError. Article is a core entity likely used as a dict key or cache key across the codebase. Also applies to BibData.extra (line 229) and User.reputation (line 272).

summary: extra['review'] raises bare KeyError if the 'review' key is missing — no friendly error message, no .get() guard
failure_scenario: If a caller omits the 'review' key from extra (or misspells it), the function crashes with uninformative KeyError: 'review'. The same pattern appears at lines 75 (extra['content']) and 76, 90 (extra['article']).

summary: type: ignore[assignment] on extra['review'] (and lines 75, 76, 90) suppresses a real type error — extra is dict[str, object], so the value is object, not Review/Article/str
failure_scenario: If extra['review'] holds something other than a Review (e.g., a misconfigured caller passes a str or a dict), the type system never catches it. The error surfaces later as an AttributeError or TypeError far from the source, making debugging harder.

summary: Extra = dict[str, object] forces callers to type: ignore every key access — there is no schema enforcement for the extra dict
failure_scenario: Every action_* function must document (in docstrings only) which keys it expects. There is no type-level contract for extra.shape. A mismatched or misspelled key passes all static checks and only fails at runtime with a raw KeyError or an opaque type error downstream.

summary: Review.scores is a mutable dict inside @dataclass(frozen=True) — the frozen decorator only prevents field rebinding, not dict mutation
failure_scenario: Code that depends on Review being fully immutable (e.g., caching, defensive copies, equality checks) can be silently broken by review.scores['clarity'] = 5.0. The object's hash also changes after mutation if the object were hashable, breaking dict/set invariants.

summary: BibData.extra: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict) in a frozen dataclass — same mutable-dict-in-frozen issue
failure_scenario: bib_data.extra['doi'] = '10.xxx' works despite frozen=True. The same unhashable-type problem applies: any BibData instance with a non-empty extra dict cannot be hashed.
